q: Heat is the form of energy that can be transferred from one system to another as a result of temperature difference.
Heat transfer and thermodynamics are two complementary branches of science.
Thermodynamics deals with equilibrium states by outlining the relationship between energy and work.
One of the basic requirement for heat transfer is the presence of a temperature difference.
There can be net heat transfer between two systems that are at the same temperature.
In fluids, heat is mainly transferred by convection, in which the motion of the fluid itself carries heat from one place to another.
In solid, heat is transferred by conduction process in which heat energy is transferred among the adjacent parts of the material without the motion of the material itself.
Heat radiation occurs in the form of electromagnetic waves.
The individual parts of a medium do not move as a whole in heat conduction.
Heat energy is transferred by successive distribution among the adjacent parts in heat conduction.
Metals generally are very good conductors of heat because they have many free electrons.
The handle end of a spoon in a hot cup of tea becomes hot by successive distribution of heat energy among the adjacent parts.
The rate of heat conduction through a material of cross-sectional area $A$ is proportional to the thickness $l$ of the material.
Thermal conductivity $k$ is a measure of the ability of a material to conduct heat.
In SI units, $k$ is watt per metre per kelvin ($W m^{-1} K^{-1}$).
The ratio $\frac{T_2-T_1}{l}$ is called temperature gradient.
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow per unit area is proportional to the magnitude of temperature gradient.
Negative sign in Fourier's law indicates that temperature $T$ must decrease as distance $x$ increases.
In the SI units, heat flux density is watt per metre squared ($W m^{-2}$).
Heat current $H$ is measured in watt ($W$).
A high value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal insulator.
Materials such as copper and silver that are good electric conductors are also good heat conductors.
Rubber, wood, and styrofoam are poor conductors of heat and have low conductivity values.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a fluid from places of higher temperature to places of lower temperature by movement of the fluid itself.
Convection currents are set up in the fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is not as dense as the cooler part.
Convection occurs only in a liquid or a gas, never in a solid.
Buoyant force is what drives natural convection.
The basic equation for the rate of convection heat transfer is known as Newton's law of cooling.
In the equation $H_{conv} = q A (T_2-T_1)$, $q$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient.
In SI units, $q$ is expressed watt per metre squared per kelvin ($W m^{-2} K^{-1}$).
When $T_2 < T_1$, heat is gained by the object and $H_{conv}$ is positive.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that does not require a material medium.
Energy transfer by radiation is the fastest (at the speed of light) and it suffers no attenuation in a vacuum.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute zero emit thermal radiation.
Thermal radiation differs from other forms of electromagnetic radiation such as X-rays that are not related to temperature.
